Clarity.justification: The paper is well-structured with clear sections, pre-registered hypotheses (H1-H3), and explicit methodology. Figures (e.g., delta reliability bar, Spearman rho heatmap) and tables effectively communicate results. However, some sections (e.g., fallback checkpoint caveats, shot-count asymmetry in H3) require careful reading to avoid confusion.

Novelty.justification: The work provides the first pre-registered mechanistic discrimination among scale inflation (H1), boundary restructuring (H2), and framing susceptibility (H3) for alignment-induced miscalibration. It extends prior observations (Xie et al. 2024, Li et al. 2024) with controlled Pythia experiments and reusable diagnostics (Spearman rho thresholds, argmax partition).

Soundness.justification: Methods are appropriate (lm-eval-harness, Brier decomposition, bootstrap CIs), but soundness is undermined by reliance on public fallback checkpoints (not RLHFlow-matched), incomplete execution of H-M4, and inconsistencies (e.g., negative Δmargin for 6.9B-PPO contradicts H2 dominance claims). Code logs show errors and truncated runs; some results (DPO > PPO ordering) are labeled exploratory without matched-training replication.

Significance.justification: Addresses an important trustworthiness issue (calibration degradation under RLHF). Findings imply that temperature scaling/ATS may be insufficient for H2-type shifts. However, results are restricted to Pythia 1.4B-6.9B; broader impact is limited without validation on production-scale families (LLaMA, Mistral).

strengths: Pre-registered hypotheses and causal design using Pythia ladder
Clear mechanistic diagnostics (Spearman rho, argmax partition, cross-benchmark H3 test)
weaknesses: Fallback checkpoints and incomplete experiments reduce reproducibility
Some conclusions (H2 dominance) weakened by contradictory results (e.g., 6.9B-PPO)
